criteria.name: Situation Classification
criteria.1: No classification attempted, wrong sub-framework selected, or classification based on superficial criteria without checking indicators
criteria.3: Correct primary classification, decision tree followed, most indicators checked, rationale stated
criteria.5: All relevant indicators systematically evaluated, correct primary and any secondary classifications identified, multiple-classification overlap handled, clear documentation of which sub-framework applies and why

criteria.name: Sub-Framework Selection
criteria.1: Applied standard DCF without recognizing the special situation, or selected sub-framework inconsistent with classification
criteria.3: Correct sub-framework selected and applied, model inputs identified, basic calculations performed
criteria.5: Sub-framework precisely matched to situation, all model-specific inputs estimated with clear sourcing, calculations transparent and reproducible, alternative sub-frameworks considered and rejected with justification

criteria.name: Model-Specific Adjustments
criteria.1: No adjustments made beyond standard DCF, or adjustments applied mechanically without justification
criteria.3: Key adjustments applied (margin convergence, total beta, liquidity discount, or excess return model), most assumptions stated, some sourcing provided
criteria.5: All required adjustments applied with full justification, assumptions explicitly stated and sourced (peer margins, regression R-squared, restricted stock studies, regulatory capital ratios), sensitivity to adjustment assumptions tested

criteria.name: Probability Weighting / Discount Application
criteria.1: No probability weighting or discounts applied when required, or arbitrary round numbers used without grounding
criteria.3: Failure probability, liquidity discount, or option pricing applied with stated rationale, some supporting evidence cited
criteria.5: Probability weighting or discounts grounded in observable data (cash runway, Z-score, restricted stock regressions, CDS spreads), estimation methodology transparent, sensitivity analysis on the discount/probability assumptions included

criteria.name: Standard vs Adjusted Comparison
criteria.1: No comparison to standard DCF, reader cannot assess the magnitude or direction of adjustments
criteria.3: Standard DCF value shown alongside adjusted value, overall impact quantified, direction of adjustment clear
criteria.5: Complete side-by-side comparison with standard DCF, each adjustment quantified as percentage of standard value, narrative explaining why each adjustment increases or decreases value, reader can evaluate each adjustment independently

criteria.name: Target Margin Sourcing (High-Growth)
criteria.1: Target margin is a guess, uses management guidance without cross-check, or is missing entirely for negative-earnings firms
criteria.3: Target margin sourced from mature peer companies, at least 3 peers cited, median or quartile range referenced
criteria.5: Target margin from 5+ mature peers with revenue and margin data shown, 25th-75th percentile range documented, selected target justified relative to company's competitive position, convergence path and timeline explicitly modeled

criteria.name: Option Pricing Accuracy (Distressed)
criteria.1: Black-Scholes not applied when equity-as-call-option is appropriate, or inputs are missing or internally inconsistent
criteria.3: Black-Scholes applied with all five inputs estimated, firm value volatility derived from a stated approach, calculation steps shown
criteria.5: All Black-Scholes inputs carefully estimated and sourced, firm value volatility uses asset-side (not equity) volatility with methodology explained, d1/d2/N(d1)/N(d2) shown, interpretation of N(d2) as solvency probability discussed, limitations of the model acknowledged

criteria.name: Total Beta / Liquidity Discount (Private)
criteria.1: Standard market beta used without considering owner diversification, no liquidity discount applied for private firm
criteria.3: Total beta calculated from market beta and correlation, liquidity discount applied from a stated range, owner context identified
criteria.5: Total beta derived from comparable firm regression data with R-squared cited, owner diversification status explicitly evaluated with consequences for beta choice, liquidity discount calibrated to company characteristics (revenue, profitability, block size) using regression rather than flat percentage

criteria.name: Excess Return Model (Financial Services)
criteria.1: FCFF model applied to a financial services firm (debt treated as financial rather than operational), or equity valued by subtracting debt from firm value
criteria.3: Equity-only model used (excess return, DDM, or FCFE), ROE and cost of equity estimated, book value anchors the valuation
criteria.5: Excess return model with year-by-year projections of ROE vs cost of equity, regulatory capital constraints checked, growth in book equity consistent with retention and ROE, P/BV interpretation provided, stable-period ROE convergence justified

criteria.name: Overall Plausibility
criteria.1: Final value is implausible (negative equity value for a solvent firm, P/BV of 50x for a bank, value exceeds industry TAM), key sanity checks missed
criteria.3: Final value passes basic reasonableness checks, comparable to peer valuations, key metrics (P/BV, EV/Revenue, implied growth) within plausible ranges
criteria.5: Final value cross-checked against multiple benchmarks (peer multiples, recent transactions, market cap for public comparables), implied assumptions reverse-engineered and verified as plausible, key risks to the valuation identified, sensitivity table shows value across range of assumptions

key_requirements: Revenue-based DCF with margin convergence path from current (negative) to target (positive peer-derived) margin
Sales-to-capital ratio used for reinvestment instead of CapEx/WC estimation
Target margin from 5+ mature peer companies with data shown
Failure probability grounded in cash runway, Z-score, or industry base rates
NOL carryforward treatment addressed if applicable
Standard DCF comparison showing impact of failure adjustment
common_pitfalls: Using management's projected margins instead of realized margins from mature peers
Applying growth rate to negative earnings (making them more negative) instead of using revenue-based approach
Forgetting to apply failure probability adjustment
Setting sales-to-capital ratio without industry benchmarking
Assuming NOLs have no value (they shelter future income from taxes)

key_requirements: Black-Scholes model with all five inputs estimated and sourced
Firm value volatility estimated from asset-side data (not equity volatility)
Face value of debt correctly aggregated across tranches
Weighted average debt maturity calculated from debt schedule
Cash flow yield (dividend yield equivalent) estimated as FCFF / Firm Value
N(d2) interpreted as solvency probability
common_pitfalls: Using equity volatility instead of firm value (asset) volatility -- overstates sigma due to leverage
Ignoring the cash flow yield (y) adjustment, which accounts for cash leaving the firm before debt maturity
Applying Black-Scholes to firms with very short debt maturity (little optionality)
Forgetting that higher volatility increases equity value in distress (more upside potential)
Not accounting for debt covenants that may force earlier liquidation than stated maturity

key_requirements: Owner diversification status explicitly determined before choosing beta
Total beta calculated from comparable firm regression R-squared
Liquidity discount calibrated to company characteristics, not a flat percentage
Minority discount applied only when valuing non-controlling stake
Both market-beta and total-beta values shown for comparison
common_pitfalls: Applying total beta to a diversified acquirer (PE fund, public company)
Using a flat 25% liquidity discount without adjusting for company size and profitability
Double-counting risk by applying both a higher discount rate and a separate risk discount
Forgetting to check whether minority discount is appropriate for the valuation context
Using book value instead of estimated market value for capital structure weights

key_requirements: Equity-only model used (excess return, DDM, or FCFE -- never FCFF for banks)
ROE compared to cost of equity to determine if bank creates or destroys value
Regulatory capital constraints checked and incorporated into growth assumptions
Book value of equity used as the anchor, with excess returns determining premium/discount
Price-to-book interpretation provided (P/BV > 1 if ROE > ke)
common_pitfalls: Subtracting debt from firm value (deposits are operational, not financial)
Using WACC as discount rate (only cost of equity is appropriate)
Projecting growth that violates regulatory capital requirements
Not normalizing ROE for cyclical loan loss provisions
Ignoring that bank book values are more meaningful than for non-financial firms (assets marked to market)

common_failure_modes.symptom: Applying standard FCFF DCF to a bank, or using equity-as-call-option for a company that is merely unprofitable but not distressed
common_failure_modes.detection: Check: Is the sub-framework appropriate for the company's classification? Does the model's assumptions match the company's characteristics?
common_failure_modes.fix: Use the classification decision tree. Banks and insurers always get the excess return model. Negative earnings with high growth gets revenue-based DCF. Only firms with actual default risk get equity-as-call-option.

common_failure_modes.symptom: Target margin uses management projections, analyst targets, or industry 'potential' rather than observed margins from mature peers
common_failure_modes.detection: Ask: Where does the target margin come from? Can I verify it from public data on mature companies?
common_failure_modes.fix: Source target margin exclusively from realized operating margins of 5+ mature companies in the same industry. Use the median or a justified point within the 25th-75th percentile range.

common_failure_modes.failure: Equity volatility used instead of firm value volatility
common_failure_modes.symptom: Black-Scholes inputs use observed equity return volatility (e.g., 80% annual) without de-levering, producing inflated equity values
common_failure_modes.detection: Sigma seems unusually high (>50% for a firm with moderate business risk), or equity value seems too high relative to firm value
common_failure_modes.fix: De-lever equity volatility using the capital structure, or use comparable firm asset volatility as the input.

common_failure_modes.failure: Total beta applied to diversified buyer
common_failure_modes.symptom: Private company discount is excessive because total beta (e.g., 3.5) is applied even though the buyer is a PE fund with a diversified portfolio
common_failure_modes.detection: Ask: Who is the buyer/owner? Are they diversified or undiversified?
common_failure_modes.fix: Use total beta only for undiversified owners. For PE funds, public acquirers, or diversified family offices, use market beta.

common_failure_modes.symptom: A blanket 25% discount applied regardless of company size, profitability, or context
common_failure_modes.detection: Discount is a round number with no explanation of how it was derived
common_failure_modes.fix: Use regression-based approach from restricted stock studies. Calibrate discount to company revenue, profitability, and block size. Larger, more profitable companies warrant smaller discounts.

common_failure_modes.failure: Debt subtracted from firm value for a bank
common_failure_modes.symptom: FCFF model applied to bank, then deposits subtracted from firm value to get equity -- produces nonsensical result
common_failure_modes.detection: Check if 'debt' in the equity bridge includes operational liabilities (deposits, insurance reserves, trading liabilities)
common_failure_modes.fix: For financial services firms, use equity-only models. Value equity directly using excess return model, DDM, or FCFE. Do not compute firm value and subtract debt.

common_failure_modes.failure: No failure probability adjustment for high-growth firms
common_failure_modes.symptom: Pre-revenue or cash-burning startup valued at billions with no acknowledgment of failure risk
common_failure_modes.detection: Check: Is there a probability-weighted scenario for failure? Is the distress sale value estimated?
common_failure_modes.fix: Estimate failure probability from cash runway, industry base rates, and company-specific factors. Apply probability weighting: Value = (1-P) x DCF + P x Distress_Value.

common_failure_modes.symptom: Reader cannot assess how much the special-situation adjustments changed the value or whether they are reasonable
common_failure_modes.detection: No standard DCF benchmark is shown alongside the adjusted value
common_failure_modes.fix: Always show: standard DCF value, each adjustment and its magnitude, final adjusted value. This allows the reader to evaluate each adjustment independently.
